perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information does not include information t hat (i) was in the Receiving Party’s possession prior to receiving the Confidential Information from the Disclosing Party; or (ii) is or later becomes generally available to the public through no fault of the Receiving Party; or (iii) Receiving Party devel ops internally without benefit of or reference to Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information (burden of proving independent development shall be on the Receiving Party) ; or (iv) Receiving Party receives from a third party who has the right to disclose suc h information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information does not include information that (i) was in the Receiving Party’s possession prior to receiving the Confidential Information from the Disclosing Party and was explicitly documented as non-confidential; or (ii) is or later becomes generally available to the public through an intentional act of the Receiving Party; or (iii) Receiving Party develops internally with vague reference to Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information; or (iv) Receiving Party receives from a third party who is believed to have the right to disclose such information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by changing the conditions under which information is excluded from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. Specifically, (i) adds a condition that information in possession must be explicitly documented as non-confidential, contradicting the original intent. (ii) changes 'no fault' to 'intentional act', (iii) changes 'without benefit of or reference' to 'with vague reference', and (iv) changes 'has the right' to 'is believed to have the right'. This creates uncertainty, especially regarding the standard of care for assessing third-party rights to disclose, potentially conflicting with section 3 regarding the receiving party's obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt measures to protect the confidentiality, limited use and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information at least as restrictive as those it adopts for its own confidential information of similar importance and in any event, no less than reasonable; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to only those of its directors, officers, agents, employees, corporate affiliates and third parties retained by the Receiving Party who have a need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof and who are made aware of the confidentiality provisions of this Agreement ( and in the case of a third party, who have signed a binding undertaking of confidentiality and non -use substantially equivalent to this Agreement ), and (iii) to not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized u se or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces several key changes that create contradictions with the contradicted text. Specifically, changing 'no fault' to 'intentional act' regarding public availability of confidential information alters the receiving party's responsibility. The Receiving Party's obligations in the 'Contradicted Text' requires a 'reasonable' level of protection and responsibility for unauthorized disclosures. The 'Changed Text' introduces the idea that the Receiving Party can intentionally release confidential information, and still have it fall outside the definition of Confidential Information. The standard regarding third parties 'who has the right' versus 'who is believed to have the right' to disclose information also lowers the standard of care which is inconsistent with the obligation in the contradicted text to protect the confidentiality of information and creates uncertainty, making it a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt measures to protect the confidentiality, limited use and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information at least as restrictive as those it adopts for its own confidential information of similar importance and in any event, no less than reasonable; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to only those of its directors, officers, agents, employees, corporate affiliates and third parties retained by the Receiving Party who have a need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof and who are made aware of the confidentiality provisions of this Agreement ( and in the case of a third party, who have signed a binding undertaking of confidentiality and non -use substantially equivalent to this Agreement ), and (iii) to not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized u se or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt measures to protect the confidentiality, limited use and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information to a reasonable extent; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to all of its directors, officers, agents, employees, corporate affiliates and third parties retained by the Receiving Party regardless of need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof and who are made aware of the confidentiality provisions of this Agreement ( and in the case of a third party, who have signed a binding undertaking of confidentiality and non -use substantially equivalent to this Agreement ), and (iii) to use Confidential Information for any purpose related or unrelated permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall not be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.explanation: This modified text contradicts itself by changing the Receiving Party's responsibilities regarding confidential information. It weakens the protection measures from 'at least as restrictive as those it adopts for its own confidential information...no less than reasonable' to 'a reasonable extent'. It also mandates disclosure to all parties regardless of need, conflicting with the original 'need to know' clause, and allows use for any purpose, related or unrelated, going against the limited use described in paragraph 4. Finally, it absolves the Receiving Party of responsibility for unauthorized disclosures, directly contradicting the original clause that made them responsible. This makes the text inconsistent and unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt measures to protect the confidentiality, limited use and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information to a reasonable extent; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to all of its directors, officers, agents, employees, corporate affiliates and third parties retained by the Receiving Party regardless of need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof and who are made aware of the confidentiality provisions of this Agreement ( and in the case of a third party, who have signed a binding undertaking of confidentiality and non -use substantially equivalent to this Agreement ), and (iii) to use Confidential Information for any purpose related or unrelated permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall not be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces several strong contradictions. It weakens confidentiality protection, broadens disclosure obligations irrespective of need, allows for any use of confidential information (related or unrelated to Paragraph 4), and absolves the Receiving Party of responsibility for unauthorized disclosures. The contradicted text simply repeats the changes, but the explanation highlights the direct conflict these changes have with the original obligations, making it a strong in-text contradiction as the original text and contradicted text have significant differences. Therefore, there's a clear contradiction created by the modification.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evaluating, negotiating and, in its discr etion, entering into the proposed Transaction. A Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information pursuant to a request by an applicable regulatory authority, or if they are required by law or pursuant to an order of a court of competent jurisdiction to disclose Confidential Information, provided, however, in such event the Receiving Party shall (i) promptly notify the Disclosing Party; (ii) consult with the Disclosing Party as to the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request; and ( iii) if so requested, cooperate with the Disclosing Party in seeking a protective or der or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evaluating, negotiating and, in its discretion, entering into the proposed Transaction. The Receiving Party must disclose Confidential Information pursuant to any request, regardless if it is from a regulatory authority, or if they are required by law or any kind of order of a court of competent jurisdiction to disclose Confidential Information, the Receiving Party (i) is not required to notify the Disclosing Party; (ii) is not required to consult with the Disclosing Party as to the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request; and (iii) is not required to cooperate with the Disclosing Party in seeking a protective order or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by removing the Receiving Party's obligation to notify and consult with the Disclosing Party when disclosing information under legal or regulatory pressure. This directly conflicts with the original requirement to notify, consult, and cooperate, potentially undermining the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party may use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evaluating, negotiating and, in its discretion, entering into the proposed Transaction. The Receiving Party must disclose Confidential Information pursuant to any request, regardless if it is from a regulatory authority, or if they are required by law or any kind of order of a court of competent jurisdiction to disclose Confidential Information, the Receiving Party (i) is not required to notify the Disclosing Party; (ii) is not required to consult with the Disclosing Party as to the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request; and (iii) is not required to cooperate with the Disclosing Party in seeking a protective order or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts itself by first stating the Receiving Party 'must disclose Confidential Information pursuant to any request' and then stating that the Receiving Party 'is not required to notify the Disclosing Party', which fundamentally alters the procedure outlined in the original text. This change is a clear inconsistency, as defined in the Perturbation Definitions, creating an in-text contradiction. The Contradicted Text is the same as the Changed Text. The removal of the obligation to notify, consult, and cooperate creates a direct conflict within the document regarding the Receiving Party's responsibilities when disclosing confidential information, thus there is a clear in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall promptly advise each other in writing if they learn of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any of their directors, officers, employees, corporate affiliates, agents or by any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties may, at their sole discretion, advise each other in writing if they learn of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any of their directors, officers, employees, corporate affiliates, agents or by any third party.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text creates an in-text contradiction by changing the obligation to report unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information from a mandatory action ('shall promptly advise') to a discretionary one ('may, at their sole discretion, advise'). This creates uncertainty about whether the parties are required to report breaches, conflicting with the intent of protecting confidential information, undermining section 6 regarding remedies for breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of a breach of this Agreement, monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy and in addition to any other legal remedy , the non -breaching Party shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including injunctive relief. In the event that any Party takes legal action as a result of a breach of this Agreement, the non -breaching Party shall be entitled to recover reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred in connection with such legal action in the event that it prevails in one or more of its claims.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall promptly advise' to 'may, at their sole discretion, advise' introduces a significant inconsistency regarding the obligation to report unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information. This directly contradicts the purpose of the agreement, as described in the perturbation explanation, and undermines the ability to seek remedies for breach, as outlined in the 'Contradicted Text'. The contradicted text describes the availability of equitable remedies and legal fees in the event of a breach of the agreement, but if parties are not obligated to report breaches, the remedies available would be very difficult to utilize. This creates an in-text contradiction because the 'Changed Text' impacts the enforceability of the 'Contradicted Text'.
